Title: A microbial biomanufacturing platform for natural and semisynthetic opiates

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: W303α genomic source for targeting sequence context; CNE1 reference from yeast
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GPD constitutive promoter in microscopy vectors; applied as fusion tag to COR1.3
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: 28-aa transmembrane domain from calnexin (CNE1) used as ER routing tag
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: When fused to COR1.3, redirected the enzyme to the ER and increased morphine specificity while reducing neomorphine branch flux
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Fusion-protein localization by confocal microscopy; metabolite profiling of ER-localized COR1.3 strains
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The ER1 tag from CNE1 successfully localized GFP and COR1.3 to the ER. 2. ER-localized COR1.3 increased morphine titer and specificity. 3. Spatial engineering using this localization element helped suppress the non-target neomorphine branch.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Thebaine feeding in engineered yeast cultures
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Observed effects may partly reflect reduced COR1.3 activity rather than purely spatial separation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful spatial-engineering tool to control pathway branch flux and improve product specificity in microbial metabolic engineering
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a catalytic or regulatory gene controlling transcription, but a core engineering element directly tested in Results for metabolic flux control

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Unique yeast promoters in YAC; GPD constitutive promoter for integrated copies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in engineered yeast strains during cultivation with thebaine
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Neopinone/codeinone pathway intermediate(s) via O-demethylation; direct unstable product neopinone discussed
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes oxidative O-demethylation of thebaine in a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent reaction, generating the intermediate that can rearrange toward codeinone or be reduced to neopine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate precursor pool for codeine, morphine, hydrocodone, oxycodone, hydromorphone
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important control point but not established as sole rate-limiting step; increased copy number with CODM improved overall flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables conversion of thebaine into downstream opioids; higher copy number contributes to higher target opioid production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; gene copy-number titration; metabolite profiling by HPLC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. T6ODM was required to initiate the engineered conversion of thebaine to downstream opioids in yeast. 2. Together with COR and CODM, it enabled codeine and morphine formation from thebaine. 3. Increased T6ODM copy number combined with elevated CODM improved morphine titer, and T6ODM plus morB alone supported hydrocodone/oxycodone production.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Thebaine feeding; supplementation with MSG and 2-oxoglutarate; 30 °C culture; batch fermentation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Its product can enter an undesired neopine/neomorphine branch because the unstable intermediate is accessible to COR before spontaneous rearrangement
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key entry enzyme for microbial opioid biomanufacturing; should be stacked with optimized CODM and downstream enzymes, with flux-balancing and branching control strategies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Requires 2-oxoglutarate as cosubstrate; product distribution is strongly influenced by spontaneous rearrangement and downstream enzyme context

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Unique yeast promoters in YAC; GPD constitutive promoter for integrated copies; C-terminal ER1-tagged version also expressed
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in yeast; either cytoplasmic or ER-targeted
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasm when untagged; endoplasmic reticulum when fused to ER1 tag
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Codeinone-to-codeine step and competing neopinone-to-neopine branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes reversible NADPH-dependent reduction of codeinone to codeine and in engineered yeast also reduces neopinone to neopine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product precursor and diverts flux toward undesired byproduct branch
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-control step rather than proven rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports codeine/morphine production but also causes flux diversion to neopine/neomorphine; ER localization increases morphine specificity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression; isoform selection; copy-number titration; ER-targeting/spatial engineering; confocal microscopy; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COR1.3 was selected among COR isoforms because of highest reported affinity for codeinone. 2. In yeast it reduced both codeinone and unexpectedly neopinone, creating an undesired neopine/neomorphine branch. 3. Increasing COR copy number shifted product ratio toward neomorphine. 4. ER localization of COR1.3 increased morphine specificity from 44% to 86% and increased morphine titer.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Thebaine feeding; optimized medium with MSG and 2-oxoglutarate; ER1 localization strategy
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher COR expression increases non-target neomorphine branch; possible reduced activity when ER localized; may also contribute to 14-hydroxycodeine formation in fermentation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A critical enzyme to tune for product specificity; spatial separation/localization engineering is valuable to suppress byproduct formation in microbial production systems
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors note COR activity on neopinone had not been clearly reported previously and may explain neopine formation

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Unique yeast promoters in YAC; GPD constitutive promoter for integrated copies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in engineered yeast strains during thebaine conversion
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes oxidative O-demethylation of codeine to morphine and of neopine to neomorphine in a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent reaction
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product morphine and also undesired neomorphine byproduct
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key bottleneck/control step; higher copy number increased morphine and total opioid titers, and discussion suggests CODM activity may be limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes morphine accumulation; increased copy number improves total flux and morphine specificity because CODM favors codeine over neopine, but limited CODM activity constrains downstream morphine/hydromorphone production
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression; copy-number titration; pathway conversion-efficiency analysis; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CODM enabled demethylation of codeine to morphine in yeast. 2. It also demethylated neopine to neomorphine, revealing an undesired branch. 3. Increasing CODM copy number raised both morphine and total end-product titers and shifted selectivity toward morphine because CODM prefers codeine as substrate. 4. Bench-scale fermentation indicated CODM activity remained a bottleneck for morphine/hydromorphone production.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Thebaine feeding; MSG and 2-oxoglutarate supplementation; batch fermentation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Although higher expression is beneficial, CODM still channels some flux to neomorphine and may remain limiting downstream of codeine
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime target for copy-number optimization or enzyme engineering to improve morphine and hydromorphone production; useful in stacked designs with T6ODM and controlled COR/morA activity
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Requires 2-oxoglutarate; authors infer codeine-to-morphine step as a pathway bottleneck in final strains

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in engineered yeast for semi-synthetic opioid production
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Semi-synthetic opioid transformation branch of morphinan alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Morphine; codeinone; hydrocodone; hydromorphone; likely 14-hydroxycodeinone-related intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Morphinone-related products and codeine; dihydrocodeine; dihydromorphine-associated conversions reported as side activities
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes NADP+-dependent oxidation/reduction transformations in opioid interconversion; in yeast it can substitute for COR to reduce codeinone to codeine and contributes to semisynthetic opioid interconversions
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms target-product precursors and also affects side-product formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as rate-limiting; activity influences branch choice and hydromorphone route feasibility
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enabled alternative route to hydrocodone/hydromorphone and could replace COR for morphine production, but also increased side-product accumulation and was constrained by cofactor supply
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway substitution experiments; heterologous expression with/without morB; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. morA from P. putida was introduced to extend yeast opioid biosynthesis toward semi-synthetic opioids. 2. Replacing COR1.3 with morA still supported morphine production with 69% selectivity. 3. In combination with morB, morA enabled hydrocodone and hydromorphone production. 4. morA activity also contributed to side products and increased neopine/neomorphine accumulation in the hydromorphone strain.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Thebaine feeding; NADP+/NADPH cofactor limitation affected hydromorphone biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can increase side-product formation including neopine/neomorphine and dihydro-opioid derivatives; cofactor limitation constrains performance
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for constructing alternative semisynthetic opioid branches, but should be paired with cofactor engineering and possibly spatial/temporal control to suppress byproducts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper describes morA as NADP+-dependent morphine dehydrogenase and notes activity on multiple opioid substrates

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in engineered yeast for semi-synthetic opioid production
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: α/β-barrel flavoprotein oxidoreductase; NADH-dependent morphinone reductase
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Semi-synthetic opioid transformation branch of morphinan alkaloid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Morphinone/codeinone-related opioid enones including codeinone and 14-hydroxycodeinone-related intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes reductive transformation of opioid ketone/enone intermediates to semi-synthetic opioid products including hydrocodone and hydromorphone; also associated with oxycodone formation from 14-hydroxycodeinone-related intermediate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target products hydrocodone, hydromorphone, and oxycodone
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: High-activity product-forming step in simplified pathway; morBE160G variant redirected flux and limited hydrocodone side production in hydromorphone route
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enabled semi-synthetic opioid production in yeast; with T6ODM alone gave highest titers of hydrocodone and oxycodone in the simplified strain
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in combinatorial pathway designs; variant comparison with morBE160G; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. morB enabled downstream conversion to hydrocodone and hydromorphone in engineered yeast. 2. A four-gene pathway with T6ODM, CODM, morA, and morB produced hydrocodone and hydromorphone. 3. The morBE160G variant reduced hydrocodone and slightly increased hydromorphone, indicating altered substrate preference/activity. 4. A minimal pathway containing only T6ODM and morB produced the highest hydrocodone and oxycodone titers.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Thebaine feeding; NADP+/NADPH limitation noted for hydromorphone branch; batch fermentation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Hydrocodone can be further reduced to dihydrocodeine by unknown mechanism; variant selection alters product distribution
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for microbial manufacturing of semi-synthetic opioids, especially in simplified pathways minimizing competing branches; variant engineering can tune product spectrum
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: An alternative amino acid sequence with E160G (RCSB PDB 1GWJ_A) was tested and changed flux distribution

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in engineered yeast hydromorphone strain
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Variant α/β-barrel flavoprotein oxidoreductase morphinone reductase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Variant morB enzyme with altered catalytic behavior relative to wild type, shifting product distribution toward hydromorphone at the expense of hydrocodone
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target semi-synthetic opioid products
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Flux-tuning variant; may limit hydrocodone formation and redirect flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Improved routing toward hydromorphone relative to wild-type morB in the multi-enzyme pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Variant substitution comparison in engineered yeast; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The E160G morB variant was tested as an alternative catalyst in the semi-synthetic opioid pathway. 2. Compared with wild-type morB, it lowered hydrocodone titer and slightly increased hydromorphone titer. 3. The variant was used in the final hydromorphone production strain to bias flux toward hydromorphone.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Thebaine feeding in engineered yeast cultures
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced activity on codeinone likely lowers total hydrocodone output
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Illustrates value of protein engineering/allele selection to tailor opioid product profiles in microbial platforms
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutation described as Glu160Gly relative to original reported morB sequence
